location: scan_directory / wrapped find execution and handle close
title: find exit status is no longer captured, so partial scan failures are silently ignored
description: The hardened behavior requires failed scans to be reflected in the exit code. The current replacement removes the earlier wrapper that wrote find's exit status to a temp file and falls back to plain io.popen(cmd). As a result, permission errors, traversal failures, and other non-zero find exits are suppressed by `2>/dev/null` and never propagated into `counters.files_failed`. The later comment acknowledges this gap but does not actually detect it.
impact: On embedded systems with mixed permissions, the collector can report exit code 0 even though parts of the filesystem were not scanned. This breaks the documented 0/1/2 semantics and can hide incomplete collections from automation.
suggestion: Restore the wrapped execution that records find's exit status during the same run, then increment `files_failed` when the recorded status is non-zero. For example, keep the `sh -c 'find ...; echo $? >statusfile'` approach and read the status after `handle:close()`.

title: End marker failures are not counted when no scan_id was obtained
description: The script intends to treat end-marker send failures as partial failures, but it only increments `counters.files_failed` when `end_id == "" and scan_id ~= ""`. If the begin marker failed twice and `scan_id` is empty, the end marker may still fail, yet that failure is ignored for exit-code purposes. This is inconsistent with the stated hardening goal that marker failures should affect reliability reporting.
impact: A run can finish with exit code 0 even though both begin and end collection markers failed, leaving the server with incomplete or missing collection state while local automation sees success.
suggestion: Track marker send success explicitly instead of inferring it from returned scan_id. For example, make `send_collection_marker` return `(ok, scan_id)` and increment `files_failed` whenever the end marker POST fails, regardless of whether a scan_id exists.

title: nc backend misparses bracketed IPv6 endpoints
description: The nc uploader extracts host and port using `hostport:match("^([^:]+)")` and `hostport:match(":(%d+)$")`. This works for IPv4/hostnames but fails for valid URLs like `http://[2001:db8::1]:8080/...`, where the host contains multiple colons and brackets. The parsed host becomes incorrect and the connection command is malformed.
impact: Collectors configured with IPv6 server addresses will fail to upload when nc is the selected backend, even though the URL is otherwise valid.
suggestion: Add explicit parsing for bracketed IPv6 literals, e.g. detect `^%[(.-)%](?::(%d+))?$` before the hostname/IPv4 path, and pass the unbracketed address to `nc` while preserving the correct Host header.

title: Collection markers are silently skipped if no upload tool was detected earlier and only nc is available with HTTPS
description: When `config.upload_tool` is empty, marker sending performs ad-hoc tool detection. In the HTTPS + nc-only case it logs a debug message and leaves `tool` unset, causing the function to return `""` without a clear warning unless debug logging is enabled. This is especially relevant for begin markers before any uploads occur or in edge cases where detection state is not set.
impact: Operators may not realize that collection markers were never attempted over HTTPS on nc-only systems, leading to missing server-side collection state with little diagnostic output.
suggestion: Emit a warning, not debug-only output, whenever marker delivery is impossible due to backend limitations. Better yet, return an explicit failure status and reason so callers can surface it consistently.
